A dumb simple way to think about the value of an asset-management firm is:=
=09You manage some assets for clients. Say you have $1=C2=A0billion of asse=
ts under management.=09You can charge the clients some fees for managing th=
eir assets. These fees tend to be roughly proportional to the amount of ass=
ets you manage. Say you can charge 1% of assets, so you get $10 million per=
 year of fees.=09You have some expenses, including office rent and computer=
s and data and travel, but=C2=A0mostly employee compensation. A crude rule =
of thumb in financial services is that 50% of revenue goes to employees, so=
 figure there=E2=80=99s $5 million left over.=09So the firm provides a stre=
am of cash flows of roughly $5 million per year.=09Slap some multiple on th=
at: Say investors expect to earn 10% a year, so they=E2=80=99d pay $50 mill=
ion for that stream of $5 million cash flows.=09So the firm is worth $50 mi=
llion, about 5% of assets under management.=09That is: Managing $1 billion =
of money for other people, for a fee, is a franchise that creates about $50=
 million of value for you, or for people who invest in your management comp=
any.=C2=A0You could sell 10% of the management company to an investor for $=
5 million.This is all crude and oversimplified in a lot of ways. But in thi=
s dumb model, the key inputs are (1) your assets under management and (2) t=
he fees you can charge.=C2=A0Some kinds of=C2=A0asset managers run enormous=
 piles of money and charge minimal fees: BlackRock Inc., which runs lots of=
 low-fee index exchange-traded funds, manages about $10.5 trillion for inve=
stors=C2=A0and is worth about $116 billion, or a bit more than 1% of assets=
.=C2=A0Other kinds of asset managers run smaller piles of money and charge =
higher fees: KKR & Co., which runs lots of expensive private equity funds, =
manages about $578 billion for investors =E2=80=94=C2=A0about 5.5% the size=
 of BlackRock =E2=80=94=C2=A0but is worth about $89 billion, or more than 1=
5% of assets, almost=C2=A0as=C2=A0much as BlackRock. Intuitively =E2=80=94=
=C2=A0crudely, inaccurately, but intuitively =E2=80=94=C2=A0you could think=
 =E2=80=9Cwell, BlackRock probably charges about 10 basis points, so at a 1=
0x multiple that's worth 1%; KKR probably charges about 1.5%, so at a 10x m=
ultiple that=E2=80=99s worth 15%.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0That calculation is not rig=
ht, of course: Those firms will have different expenses (it=E2=80=99s proba=
bly more expensive, as a percentage of assets,=C2=A0to pay a private equity=
 dealmaker than someone making sure an ETF tracks its index), and of course=
 investors care about not just current fee income but also expected=C2=A0fu=
ture growth.Still,=C2=A0this model suggests a rough=C2=A0cap=C2=A0on the va=
lue of an asset-management firm. If you charge clients 5% a year and have n=
o expenses, then at a 10x multiple your management company should be worth =
something like 50% of assets under management.=C2=A0Here=E2=80=99s this:Hed=
ge-fund manager Bill Ackman sold a 10% stake in Pershing Square ahead of a =
planned initial public offering in a deal valuing the company at more than =
$10 billion.Investors include Arch Capital Group Ltd., BTG Pactual, Consult=
a Ltd., ICONIQ Investment Management, Menora Mivtachim Holdings and an inte=
rnational group of family offices, according to a statement Monday. The pur=
chase price was $1.05 billion. ...Ackman is planning a stock-market listing=
 for Pershing Square as soon as late 2025, a person familiar with the matte=
r said last week.Pershing Square is the management company that runs closed=
-end funds =E2=80=94 Pershing Square Holdings Ltd., which has about $15 bil=
lion in assets, and Pershing Square USA Ltd., which he intends to list on t=
he New York Stock Exchange =E2=80=94 as well as unlisted hedge funds.Pershi=
ng Square's total assets under management =E2=80=94=C2=A0Pershing Square Ho=
ldings plus the hedge funds =E2=80=94=C2=A0come to something like $18 billi=
on. A firm that manages $18 billion for other people is worth $10 billion f=
or its owners. That=E2=80=99s =E2=80=A6=C2=A0impressive? A crude way to thi=
nk about it is that the market thinks that, in the long run, Pershing Squar=
e will be able to extract about half the value of its clients=E2=80=99 port=
folios for itself.
  [1]
Seems high! But that=E2=80=99s probably not really the right way to think a=
bout it. Instead, this is probably more of a bet that, in the future, Persh=
ing Square=E2=80=99s clients=E2=80=99 portfolios will be much bigger. There=
=E2=80=99s other Pershing Square news this week:Bill Ackman=E2=80=99s Persh=
ing Square aims to raise $25 billion for a new closed-end fund expected to =
draw interest from US retail investors, which would more than double the fe=
e-paying assets the firm manages, according to people with direct knowledge=
 of the plans.The vehicle, known as Pershing Square USA Ltd., has filed pla=
ns to list on the New York Stock Exchange, and will significantly amplify t=
he management company=E2=80=99s revenue ahead of a potential initial public=
 offering, said the people, who asked not to be identified because no targe=
t has been made public. For the US closed-end fund, Pershing plans to charg=
e a 2% management fee, which would be waived for the first 12 months, and n=
o incentive fee, filings show.The expected asset growth enabled Pershing Sq=
uare to nab a valuation of $10.5 billion in a stake sale announced Monday. =
The firm already has a European closed-end fund, Pershing Square Holdings L=
td., which has about $15 billion in assets, among other vehicles.We talked =
about Pershing Square USA (=E2=80=9CPSUS=E2=80=9D) a few months ago, and I =
pointed out a slide in an investor presentation that suggested PSUS might r=
aise $10 billion. I put a little =E2=80=9C(!)=E2=80=9D after that number, b=
ecause it seemed ambitious, but now the target is $25 billion. The investor=
s in Pershing Square aren=E2=80=99t paying $1 billion for 10% of a firm tha=
t manages $18 billion.=C2=A0They=E2=80=99re paying $1 billion for 10% of a =
firm that they expect to manage more than twice that in the near future, an=
d to grow from there.More generally, I think, they are betting on Bill Ackm=
an as a model for a hedge fund manager=C2=A0and Pershing Square as a model =
for a hedge fund management firm. The Wall Street Journal notes:[A Pershing=
 Square]=C2=A0listing, should one happen, would be an anomaly in the world =
of hedge funds. After a burst of debuts just before the 2008-09 financial c=
risis, public markets soured on hedge-fund firms. The management and perfor=
mance fees that make up hedge funds=E2=80=99 revenues can be unpredictable,=
 assets can shrink if investors yank their money out, and returns can be vo=
latile.=C2=A0But Ackman is trying to=C2=A0solve=C2=A0those problems:=09His =
fees will not be particularly unpredictable. PSUS will make all its money f=
rom management fees, not incentive fees; it will get 2% a year (after the f=
ree first year). Meanwhile, his existing European fund, Pershing Square Hol=
dings (PSH), does charge incentive fees, but it plans to reduce those fees =
as PSUS grows.
  [2]
=C2=A0Basically investors in Pershing Square, the management company, are b=
uying mostly=C2=A0predictable=C2=A0management fees.
  [3]
=09Those funds aren=E2=80=99t hedge funds, and investors can=E2=80=99t yank=
 their money out.=C2=A0Most of the existing money is in PSH, and all of the=
 new money will be=C2=A0in PSUS, and both of those are public closed-end fu=
nds whose investors can=E2=80=99t withdraw money.=C2=A0Charging 2% forever =
on a pot of tens of billions of dollars of permanent capital is quite possi=
bly a better business than charging 1.5% of assets and 20% of performance o=
n hedge funds whose investors can withdraw money, or on private equity fund=
s with limited lifespans where you need to raise new funds periodically.My =
view has long been that the essential skill of a hedge fund manager is not=
=C2=A0picking the right stocks, but rather=C2=A0continuing to run a hedge f=
und:=C2=A0Picking the right stocks is a good way to keep running a hedge fu=
nd, but it is not the only one, and locking up capital permanently is in so=
me ways even better. But that is even more true for outside investors: They=
 are looking to own a company for the long term, and they can=E2=80=99t be =
sure that you will always pick the right stocks. But if they=E2=80=99re sur=
e that your capital can=E2=80=99t leave, that helps.This predictability =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0of assets and fees =E2=80=94=C2=A0is not the only thing to Pers=
hing Square=E2=80=99s pitch. There=E2=80=99s also the cost structure. Lots =
of multi-strategy multi-manager hedge funds achieve high returns, high fees=
 and growing assets by hiring the best portfolio managers, allocating capit=
al to them rigorously, supervising them closely, firing them if they underp=
erform, and paying them gigantic amounts of money.Pershing Square =E2=80=A6=
=C2=A0buys 12 stocks? It periodically discloses its portfolio, which is sma=
ll and slow to change. =E2=80=9CPershing Square owns basically a long-only =
portfolio of large-cap US stocks,=E2=80=9D I wrote in February. =E2=80=9CPe=
rhaps no other hedge fund manager has perfected the art of having their mon=
ey work for them while they sleep =E2=80=94 or tweet =E2=80=94 like Bill Ac=
kman,=E2=80=9D wrote Bloomberg=E2=80=99s Tom Maloney and Hema Parmar, about=
 Ackman=E2=80=99s performance and his portfolio of (at the time) 10 stocks.=
That's cheap! It=E2=80=99s cheap in the sense that you can run Pershing Squ=
are with a relatively small staff; you only need so many analysts to pick 1=
2 stocks. It=E2=80=99s also cheap in the sense that you can scale up easily=
: If PSUS really does raise $25 billion of new money, it will just put that=
 money into the same 12 stocks. It won=E2=80=99t take any more analytical w=
ork (they=E2=80=99ve already picked the stocks), and it probably won=E2=80=
=99t be that expensive (these are liquid large-cap stocks so the trades hav=
e more capacity). Charging 2% management fees on a low-turnover portfolio o=
f large liquid public stocks seems like a better business model than chargi=
ng 2% and picking new stocks each day.Now, two points here. One is that Per=
shing Square is not=C2=A0just=C2=A0a portfolio of 12 large-cap stocks. It=
=C2=A0is=C2=A0a hedge fund and it does sometimes make big bets using deriva=
tives that can=C2=A0make it a lot of money. We talked in 2020 about a credi=
t default swap index trade=C2=A0in which Ackman apparently paid $27 million=
 for a bet that paid off $2.6 billion in the early days of Covid. Ackman=E2=
=80=99s pitch to investors in PSUS is not just =E2=80=9CI=E2=80=99ll buy 12=
 nice stocks for you=E2=80=9D; it is also =E2=80=9CI will sometimes make de=
rivatives trades that make a lot of money when markets are volatile.=E2=80=
=9D It is a hedge-fund-like pitch, not a long-only pitch. And in fact one r=
eason that Pershing Square is=C2=A0doing a US public fund now, as opposed t=
o years ago, is that US securities regulation has become more tolerant of p=
ublic mutual funds that use derivatives, so Ackman can do=C2=A0those deriva=
tives trades in PSUS.The other point is that charging 2% management fees on=
 a low-turnover portfolio of large liquid public stocks is=C2=A0such=C2=A0a=
 good business model that it presents a problem: Who would take the other s=
ide of that? Who would pay you 2% to own 12 big stocks for them? Why would =
you expect Ackman to raise $25 billion in a closed-end fund that would do t=
his? I think this is the answer, from the Journal:His =E2=80=9Cbrand-name p=
rofile and broad retail following, along with a substantial media following=
, will drive substantial investor interest,=E2=80=9D the prospectus said. O=
ne of the people familiar with the matter said Ackman plans to write about =
new investments on X once the retail fund gets approved. (He is currently b=
arred from marketing his Europe-listed fund to U.S. investors.)There is a m=
eme-stock element to all of this.=C2=A0If you=E2=80=99re investing in Ackma=
n=E2=80=99s=C2=A0management company, you are not really betting on Ackman=
=E2=80=99s ability to pick stocks and make smart derivatives trades, becaus=
e you don=E2=80=99t directly benefit from those skills. You are betting on =
Ackman=E2=80=99s ability to=C2=A0raise money by tweeting and going on telev=
ision, to build his public profile by doing controversial stuff on social m=
edia and to convert that profile into investor interest. =C2=A0

      Equities in Secaucus
   =20
 =20
I wrote yesterday about TXSE, the proposed Texas Stock Exchange, which =E2=
=80=9Cwill be entirely electronic,=E2=80=9D but will have its office in Dal=
las.=C2=A0I half-jokingly called it =E2=80=9Ca computer that trades stocks.=
 In Texas.=E2=80=9D A spokesperson for TXSE said that actually it will have=
 data centers in both Secaucus, New Jersey, and Dallas, Texas.=C2=A0One poi=
nt that I made yesterday is that, in the olden days, a person could only tr=
ade on one stock exchange, because=C2=A0a stock exchange was a building and=
 you had to stand there to trade. You can only really stand in one place at=
 a time: You could move between stations to trade different stocks, but you=
 were limited to one building. Now a stock exchange is a computer, and you =
can connect your computer to as many stock-exchange computers as you want, =
so it=E2=80=99s perfectly plausible to trade on a dozen stock exchanges sim=
ultaneously. This encourages new entrants: You don=E2=80=99t have to be the=
 biggest exchange with the most liquidity to attract traders, because the t=
raders can trade everywhere, and will trade wherever they find the best pri=
ce on any particular stock.And yet in another sense, if you are a high-spee=
d electronic stock trader, you really can only be in one place at a time, a=
nd that place is New Jersey.=C2=A0If a stock is trading at $10.00 on the Ne=
w York Stock Exchange and $10.01 on Nasdaq, you want to buy on NYSE and sel=
l on Nasdaq instantaneously. Nasdaq=E2=80=99s computer is in Carteret, New =
Jersey; NYSE=E2=80=99s is in Mahwah. BATS is in Secaucus. All of these plac=
es are fairly close to one another, and if you want to send electronic mess=
ages from one to another it doesn=E2=80=99t take very long. Sending message=
s to Dallas would take ages by comparison, and in the modern world of elect=
ronic stock trading every microsecond counts.=C2=A0So the Texas Stock Excha=
nge, like all the other stock exchanges, will be in New Jersey. Though also=
 in Texas.Elsewhere: =E2=80=9CTexan stock exchange start-up draws sceptical=
 industry response.=E2=80=9D

We talked a few months ago about a proposal for financing Ukraine. The idea=
 is roughly that (1) Ukraine has a large claim against Russia for war damag=
es, (2) G7 governments (the US, UK, Canada, France, Germany, Italy and Japa=
n) have frozen a lot of Russian assets under sanctions programs, (3) the G7=
 governments can=E2=80=99t just=C2=A0give=C2=A0that Russian money to Ukrain=
e (because=C2=A0they=C2=A0have no legal claim on it) but (4) they can=C2=A0=
lend=C2=A0money to Ukraine, collateralized by Ukraine=E2=80=99s claims agai=
nst Russia. And then if Ukraine ever successfully prosecutes that claim aga=
inst Russia, it can get the money and repay the G7 lenders. And if =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0more likely =E2=80=94=C2=A0it can=E2=80=99t, then the lenders can =
foreclose on the claim, at which point they=C2=A0will=C2=A0have a legal cla=
im against Russia for Ukraine=E2=80=99s war damages, which they can enforce=
 by taking the frozen Russian assets.It=E2=80=99s cute. But one problem wit=
h any program of lending money to Ukraine backed by seized Russian assets i=
s that the seized assets appear to have shorter terms than the loans. The F=
inancial Times reports:The US is ready to lead a loan of $50bn to Ukraine r=
epaid by profits from frozen Russian assets if the EU can indefinitely exte=
nd sanctions against Moscow, according to a leaked discussion paper.Washing=
ton needs the EU to prolong the bloc=E2=80=99s sanctions on Russian state a=
ssets, which expire every six months unless renewed by unanimous consent, u=
ntil the end of the war to ensure the US is not left on the hook for repaym=
ents.But any such change to the EU regime would require the approval of lea=
ders including Hungary=E2=80=99s Viktor Orb=C3=A1n, who has jealously guard=
ed his regular veto rights over sanctions decisions.=C2=A0A lot of problems=
 in finance involve people who have short-term funding that rolls over cons=
tantly, who start to=C2=A0treat that=C2=A0as long-term funding, and who the=
n end up surprised when one day it does not roll over. This is=C2=A0the geo=
political=C2=A0version of that.

      Cash fees
   =20
 =20
If you are a store and you accept credit cards for payment, you pay fees to=
 get your money. If you sell a thing for $100, and the buyer pays with a cr=
edit card, you get something like $97. The rest goes to the buyer=E2=80=99s=
 bank, your bank, the credit card network and other intermediaries. In exch=
ange for those fees, you get convenience, for you and the customer: The cus=
tomer gets to buy your stuff without carrying cash (so you can sell more st=
uff), and the money shows up in your bank account relatively quickly.If you=
 accept cash for payment, you don=E2=80=99t pay those fees. If you sell a t=
hing for $100, and the buyer pays cash, you put $100 in your cash register.=
 But then what? You probably don=E2=80=99t pay for stuff in cash: You don=
=E2=80=99t hand your suppliers a bag of cash; you pay them using=C2=A0bank =
money, wire transfers or checks or whatever. You have to=C2=A0turn=C2=A0the=
 customer=E2=80=99s $100 into $100 in the bank. And that is not=C2=A0free. =
You have to pay someone to walk the money over to the bank. You might have =
to pay for a security guard to escort the person walking the money over to =
the bank, or to guard the cash register until you walk it over. If you only=
 walk the money over to the bank once a week, you miss out on interest on t=
he money as it sits in the cash register.=C2=A0The bank might charge you a =
fee for depositing the money.
  [4]
So if you sell a thing for $100 in cash, you get $100 in cash, but =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0after the costs and delays associated with handling cash and turni=
ng it into bank money =E2=80=94=C2=A0that=E2=80=99s worth less than $100 of=
 bank money. I don=E2=80=99t know how much less; it will depend on the part=
iculars of your business. But it=E2=80=99s=C2=A0possible=C2=A0that it will =
be less than $97, and it will be cheaper for you to accept credit cards tha=
n cash.Here=E2=80=99s a Wall Street Journal article about businesses that c=
harge fees to accept cash:Paying with cash used to be a way to get a discou=
nt. These days it can often cost an extra $1 to $6 =E2=80=94 the sort of tr=
ansaction fees once limited to swiping a credit card or using an out-of-net=
work ATM.Reverse ATMs like those at Yankee Stadium are now common at cashle=
ss venues and restaurants across the country as a way to cater to those who=
 prefer paying in cash. People who want to pay their parking tickets, tolls=
, taxes or phone bills in cash, meanwhile, often learn that government agen=
cies and businesses have outsourced that option to companies that usually c=
harge a fee.All that can amount to a penalty on the people who prefer payin=
g cash. Though it is more common to buy things with cards and mobile device=
s, cash remains the third-most popular way to pay, accounting for 16% of al=
l payments in 2023, according to the Federal Reserve. That=E2=80=99s down 2=
 percentage points from the year before, continuing a steady decline that a=
ccelerated during the pandemic.=E2=80=9CIt=E2=80=99s unbelievable that we a=
ctually have to tell retailers, =E2=80=98This is U.S. currency and it=E2=80=
=99s something that should be accepted,=E2=80=99=E2=80=9D said Jonathan Ale=
xander, executive director of the Consumer Choice in Payment Coalition, a g=
roup of businesses and nonprofits lobbying for the continued acceptance of =
cash.A =E2=80=9Creverse ATM=E2=80=9D is a machine that accepts $200 of cash=
 and gives you back a debit card for $196.50, charging you for the cost of =
dealing with your cash. Also I like that there=E2=80=99s a cash lobby. Why =
shouldn=E2=80=99t there be? If paying cash costs retailers money, that has =
to mean it=E2=80=99s making money for somebody else.

=80=9D where a fraudster offers fake investments, pays Ponzi-like returns a=
nd =E2=80=9Cexploits the trust and friendship that exist in groups of peopl=

ome ethnic or religious group, who are more likely to trust one of their ow=
n with their money, but all sorts of common bonds can work. The more intens=

o allegedly targeted fellow Harvard MBAs for a Ponzi scheme. In some sense,=

arvard MBA, but also I kind of admire it: That=E2=80=99s an affinity group =
with a lot of trust and loyalty and also a lot of money!Anyway here=E2=80=

 lament that they were vulnerable to manipulation by someone they knew and =

 Pickleball Rocks T-shirt with the word =E2=80=98Rocks=E2=80=99 covered up.=
=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9CWhy would he take advantage of an 87-year-old friend,=E2=

utside investors, because they are more predictable and don=E2=80=99t depen=

  [4] Patrick McKenzie, my source on all things related to payments, writes=
: =E2=80=9CIf you deal with commercially relevant quantities of cash, in ad=
dition to your own labor costs associated with it, your bank will assess a =
fee for depositing it. Larger retailers will negotiate their own bespoke ra=
tes, but the right ballpark for small businesses above a nominal amount is =
3 bps, or $3 per $10,000 transacted.=E2=80=9D

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">Ackman</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A dumb simple way to think about the v=
alue of an asset-management firm is:</p><ul style=3D"list-style-type: disc;=
 padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">=
You manage some assets for clients. Say you have $1=C2=A0billion of assets =
under management.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">You can charge t=
he clients some fees for managing their assets. These fees tend to be rough=
ly proportional to the amount of assets you manage. Say you can charge 1% o=
f assets, so you get $10 million per year of fees.</li>=09<li style=3D"marg=
in-bottom: 5px;">You have some expenses, including office rent and computer=
s and data and travel, but=C2=A0<em>mostly </em>employee compensation. A cr=
ude rule of thumb in financial services is that 50% of revenue goes to empl=
oyees, so figure there=E2=80=99s $5 million left over.</li>=09<li style=3D"=
margin-bottom: 5px;">So the firm provides a stream of cash flows of roughly=
 $5 million per year.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Slap some mu=
ltiple on that: Say investors expect to earn 10% a year, so they=E2=80=99d =
pay $50 million for that stream of $5 million cash flows.</li>=09<li style=
=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">So the firm is worth $50 million, about 5% of asse=
ts under management.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">That is: Mana=
ging $1 billion of money for other people, for a fee, is a franchise that c=
reates about $50 million of value for you, or for people who invest in your=
 management company.=C2=A0You could sell 10% of the management company to a=
n investor for $5 million.</li></ul><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This is al=
l crude and oversimplified in a lot of ways. But in this dumb model, the ke=
y inputs are (1) your assets under management and (2) the fees you can char=
ge.=C2=A0Some kinds of=C2=A0asset managers run enormous piles of money and =
charge minimal fees: BlackRock Inc., which runs lots of low-fee index excha=
nge-traded funds, <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/=
35628937.287313/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9peD9kb2M9L0FyY2hpdmVzL2VkZ2FyL2R=
hdGEvMTM2NDc0Mi8wMDAwOTUwMTcwMjQwNTQxNDgvYmxrLTIwMjQwMzMxLmh0bQ/607f07ceef4=
b8524a319a23cB22a8ce00" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" targ=
et=3D"_blank" title=3D"page 33" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: n=
one !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bor=
der-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">manages about $10.5 trill=
ion for investors</a>=C2=A0and is worth about $116 billion, or a bit more t=
han 1% of assets.=C2=A0Other kinds of asset managers run smaller piles of m=
oney and charge higher fees: KKR & Co., which runs lots of expensive pr=
ivate equity funds, <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/clic=
k/35628937.287313/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuc2VjLmdvdi9peD9kb2M9L0FyY2hpdmVzL2VkZ2FyL=
2RhdGEvMTQwNDkxMi8wMDAxNDA0OTEyMjQwMDAwMTEva2tyLTIwMjQwMzMxLmh0bQ/607f07cee=
f4b8524a319a23cC2f461cde" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" ta=
rget=3D"_blank" title=3D"page 134" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration=
: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; =
border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">manages about $578 bil=
lion</a> for investors =E2=80=94=C2=A0about 5.5% the size of BlackRock =E2=
=80=94=C2=A0but is worth about $89 billion, or more than 15% of assets, alm=
ost=C2=A0as=C2=A0much as BlackRock. Intuitively =E2=80=94=C2=A0crudely, ina=
ccurately, but intuitively =E2=80=94=C2=A0you could think =E2=80=9Cwell, Bl=
ackRock probably charges about 10 basis points, so at a 10x multiple that's=
 worth 1%; KKR probably charges about 1.5%, so at a 10x multiple that=E2=80=
=99s worth 15%.=E2=80=9D=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">That calcula=
tion is not right, of course: Those firms will have different expenses (it=
=E2=80=99s probably more expensive, as a percentage of assets,=C2=A0to pay =
a private equity dealmaker than someone making sure an ETF tracks its index=
), and of course investors care about not just current fee income but also =
expected=C2=A0future growth.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Still,=C2=A0th=
is model suggests a rough=C2=A0<em>cap=C2=A0</em>on the value of an asset-m=
anagement firm. If you charge clients 5% a year and have no expenses, then =
at a 10x multiple your management company should be worth something like 50=
% of assets under management.=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><a href=
=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/35628937.287313/aHR0cHM6L=
y93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDYtMDMvcGVyc2hpbmctc3F1=
YXJlLXNlbGxzLTEwLXN0YWtlLWZvci0xLTA1LWJpbGxpb24tYWhlYWQtb2YtaXBvP2NtcGlkPUJ=
CRDA2MDYyNF9NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dG=
VyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTI0MDYwNiZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a3=
19a23cB9401f71e" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_=
blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bo=
ttom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; =
background-color: #ccc;">Here=E2=80=99s this</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Hedge-fund manager Bill Ackman sold a 10% stake in Per=
shing Square ahead of a planned initial public offering in a deal valuing t=
he company at more than $10 billion.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Invest=
ors include Arch Capital Group Ltd., BTG Pactual, Consulta Ltd., ICONIQ Inv=
estment Management, Menora Mivtachim Holdings and an international group of=
 family offices, according to a statement Monday. The purchase price was $1=
.05 billion. ...</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Ackman is planning a stock=
-market listing for Pershing Square as soon as late 2025, a person familiar=
 with the matter said last week.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Pershing S=
quare is the management company that runs closed-end funds =E2=80=94 Pershi=
ng Square Holdings Ltd., which has about $15 billion in assets, and Pershin=
g Square USA Ltd., which he intends to list on the New York Stock Exchange =
=E2=80=94 as well as unlisted hedge funds.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"marg=
in: 16px 0;">Pershing Square's total assets under management =E2=80=94=C2=
=A0Pershing Square Holdings plus the hedge funds =E2=80=94=C2=A0come to <a =
href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/35628937.287313/aHR0c=
HM6Ly9hc3NldHMucGVyc2hpbmdzcXVhcmVob2xkaW5ncy5jb20vMjAyNC8wMi8wNzExMTQwNS8y=
MDI0LUFubnVhbC1JbnZlc3Rvci1QcmVzZW50YXRpb24ucGRm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBd=
e1f4386" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" s=
tyle=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-wid=
th: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgrou=
nd-color: #ccc;">something like $18 billion</a>. A firm that manages $18 bi=
llion for other people is worth $10 billion for its owners. That=E2=80=99s =
=E2=80=A6=C2=A0impressive? A crude way to think about it is that the market=
 thinks that, in the long run, Pershing Square will be able to extract abou=
t half the value of its clients=E2=80=99 portfolios for itself.<a class=3D"=
footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decorati=
on: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000=
; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !impo=
rtant; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a></p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Seems high! But that=E2=80=99s probabl=
y not really the right way to think about it. Instead, this is probably mor=
e of a bet that, in the future, Pershing Square=E2=80=99s clients=E2=80=99 =
portfolios will be much bigger. There=E2=80=99s <a href=3D"https://link.mai=
l.bloombergbusiness.com/click/35628937.287313/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnL=
mNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDYtMDMvYWNrbWFuLXMtcGVyc2hpbmctc3F1YXJlLXRh=
cmdldHMtcmFpc2luZy0yNS1iaWxsaW9uLWZvci11cy1mdW5kP2NtcGlkPUJCRDA2MDYyNF9NT05=
FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPT=
I0MDYwNiZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBf75b2d77"=
 itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"=
color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px;=
 border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color=
: #ccc;">other Pershing Square news this week</a>:</p><blockquote><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Bill Ackman=E2=80=99s Pershing Square aims to raise $2=
5 billion for a new closed-end fund expected to draw interest from US retai=
l investors, which would more than double the fee-paying assets the firm ma=
nages, according to people with direct knowledge of the plans.</p><p style=
=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The vehicle, known as Pershing Square USA Ltd., has fi=
led plans to list on the New York Stock Exchange, and will significantly am=
plify the management company=E2=80=99s revenue ahead of a potential initial=
 public offering, said the people, who asked not to be identified because n=
o target has been made public. For the US closed-end fund, Pershing plans t=
o charge a 2% management fee, which would be waived for the first 12 months=
, and no incentive fee, filings show.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The e=
xpected asset growth enabled Pershing Square to nab a valuation of $10.5 bi=
llion in a stake sale announced Monday. The firm already has a European clo=
sed-end fund, Pershing Square Holdings Ltd., which has about $15 billion in=
 assets, among other vehicles.</p></blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"=
>We <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/35628937.28731=
3/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDItMDgvY=
mlsbC1hY2ttYW4td2FudHMteW91ci1tb25leT9jbXBpZD1CQkQwNjA2MjRfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1=
dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yNDA2MDYmdXR=
tX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBf283113d" itemprop=3D=
"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #0000=
00; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bott=
om-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">tal=
ked about</a> Pershing Square USA (=E2=80=9CPSUS=E2=80=9D) a few months ago=
, and I pointed out <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/clic=
k/35628937.287313/aHR0cHM6Ly9hc3NldHMucGVyc2hpbmdzcXVhcmVob2xkaW5ncy5jb20vM=
jAyNC8wMi8wNzExMTQwNS8yMDI0LUFubnVhbC1JbnZlc3Rvci1QcmVzZW50YXRpb24ucGRm/607=
f07ceef4b8524a319a23cCde1f4386" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemsco=
pe" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !impor=
tant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom=
-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">a slide in an investor presentation=
</a> that suggested PSUS might raise $10 billion. I put a little =E2=80=9C(=
!)=E2=80=9D after that number, because it seemed ambitious, but now the tar=
get is $25 billion. The investors in Pershing Square aren=E2=80=99t paying =
$1 billion for 10% of a firm that manages $18 billion.=C2=A0They=E2=80=99re=
 paying $1 billion for 10% of a firm that they expect to manage more than t=
wice that in the near future, and to grow from there.</p><p style=3D"margin=
: 16px 0;">More generally, I think, they are betting on Bill Ackman as a mo=
del for a hedge fund manager=C2=A0and Pershing Square as a model for a hedg=
e fund management firm. The <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.=
com/click/35628937.287313/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cud3NqLmNvbS9maW5hbmNlL2ludmVzdGluZ=

it cards for payment, you pay fees to get your money. If you sell a thing f=
or $100, and the buyer pays with a credit card, you get something like $97.=
 The rest goes to the buyer=E2=80=99s bank, your bank, the credit card netw=
ork and other intermediaries. In exchange for those fees, you get convenien=
ce, for you and the customer: The customer gets to buy your stuff without c=
arrying cash (so you can sell more stuff), and the money shows up in your b=
ank account relatively quickly.</p><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you acce=
pt cash for payment, you don=E2=80=99t pay those fees. If you sell a thing =
for $100, and the buyer pays cash, you put $100 in your cash register. But =
then what? <em>You</em> probably don=E2=80=99t pay for stuff in cash: You d=
on=E2=80=99t hand your suppliers a bag of cash; you pay them using=C2=A0<em=
>bank money</em>, wire transfers or checks or whatever. You have to=C2=A0<e=
m>turn=C2=A0</em>the customer=E2=80=99s $100 into $100 in the bank. And tha=
t is not=C2=A0<em>free</em>. You have to pay someone to walk the money over=
 to the bank. You might have to pay for a security guard to escort the pers=
on walking the money over to the bank, or to guard the cash register until =
you walk it over. If you only walk the money over to the bank once a week, =
you miss out on interest on the money as it sits in the cash register.=C2=
=A0The bank might charge you a fee for depositing the money.<a class=3D"foo=

ciated with handling cash and turning it into bank money =E2=80=94=C2=A0tha=
t=E2=80=99s worth less than $100 of bank money. I don=E2=80=99t know how mu=
ch less; it will depend on the particulars of your business. But it=E2=80=
=99s=C2=A0<em>possible=C2=A0</em>that it will be less than $97, and it will=

"margin: 16px 0;">Paying with cash used to be a way to get a discount. Thes=

<div id=3D"footnote-1" class=3D"footnote-content" style=3D"font-style: ital=
ic;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">[1] And Pershing Square Holdings has generally=
 traded at a discount to its net asset value =E2=80=94 as much as about 38%=
, though now it=E2=80=99s more like 23% =E2=80=94 which also suggests somet=
hing about the expected value capture by the management company.</p>
</div>
<div id=3D"footnote-2" class=3D"footnote-content" style=3D"font-style: ital=
ic;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">[2] See slides 15 and 16 of this <a href=3D"ht=
tps://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/35628937.287313/aHR0cHM6Ly9hc3N=
ldHMucGVyc2hpbmdzcXVhcmVob2xkaW5ncy5jb20vMjAyNC8wMi8wNzExMTQwNS8yMDI0LUFubn=
VhbC1JbnZlc3Rvci1QcmVzZW50YXRpb24ucGRm/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cDde1f4386" i=
temprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"co=
lor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; b=
order-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: =
#ccc;">February PSH presentation</a>: PSH will =E2=80=9Creceive an addition=
al fee reduction from 20% of management fees earned on any Pershing Square =
funds that invest in public securities which do not charge performance fees=
.=E2=80=9D So if PSUS does raise $25 billion and charge 2% fees, then that =
will reduce PSH=E2=80=99s performance fees by $100 million (0.4% of $25 bil=
lion).</p>
</div>
<div id=3D"footnote-3" class=3D"footnote-content" style=3D"font-style: ital=
ic;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">[3] When I <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomb=
ergbusiness.com/click/35628937.287313/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vc=
GluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjQtMDItMDgvYmlsbC1hY2ttYW4td2FudHMteW91ci1tb25leT9j=
bXBpZD1CQkQwNjA2MjRfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV=
3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yNDA2MDYmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef=
4b8524a319a23cCf283113d" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" tar=
get=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; b=
order-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style:=
 solid; background-color: #ccc;">
wrote about PSUS in February</a>, I noted that it would not charge performa=
nce fees and argued that, once you reach a certain level of fame and wealth=
, charging management fees is nicer, for you: =E2=80=9CWhen you are a matur=
e famous hedge fund manager, the performance fees are less important. You h=
ave billions of dollars of your own money in the fund now, so if your perfo=
rmance is good you will make lots of money just on the appreciation of your=
 own money. But the management fees are more attractive: You are now famous=
, so you can put out a tweet and expect to raise $10 billion of new money, =
which will pay you a $200 million fee forever. That starts to look nice.=E2=
=80=9D But of course the real analysis is that management fees are nicer fo=
r outside investors, because they are more predictable and don=E2=80=99t de=
pend on performance.</p>
</div>
<div id=3D"footnote-4" class=3D"footnote-content" style=3D"font-style: ital=
ic;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">[4] Patrick McKenzie, my source on all things =
related to payments, <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/cli=
ck/35628937.287313/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYml0c2Fib3V0bW9uZXkuY29tL2FyY2hpdmUvdGhl=
LWluZnJhc3RydWN0dXJlLWJlaGluZC1hdG1zLw/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBbfe76fec" i=
temprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"co=
lor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; b=
order-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: =
#ccc;">writes</a>: =E2=80=9CIf you deal with commercially relevant quantiti=
es of cash, in addition to your own labor costs associated with it, your ba=
nk will assess a fee for depositing it. Larger retailers will negotiate the=
ir own bespoke rates, but the right ballpark for small businesses above a n=
ominal amount is 3 bps, or $3 per $10,000 transacted.=E2=80=9D</p>
</div>
